News.title: Attending the Baftas fills me with dread, but I wish I’d been there for BBC meltdown over Tourettes slur, Clarkson says
News.content:
JEREMY Clarkson has admitted that he would have loved watching the BBC meltdown after the Tourettes saga, despite the awards show “filling him with dread”.

The former Top Gear presenter spoke out after John Davidson, who suffers from Tourettes, shouted out a racial slur during the awards ceremony.





John later voluntarily left the Baftas amid shock at his outburst as Delroy Lindo and Michael B. Jordan were on stage presenting an award.

Weighing into the debate, Clarkson said he never goes to the Baftas because the idea of spending an evening with “north London vegetablists” filled him with dread, reports the Times.

And he even claimed that in his office he keeps what he calls a “Wall of Failure” – made up of Bafta nomination certificates he has received over the years.

However, he pointed out there was not a single actual Bafta among them, saying he had never won one and never would.



Clarkson said the awards body does not give Baftas to “people like me”, comparing the idea to the Booker Prize being handed to Jeffrey Archer.

But the Top Gear legend said he “wished he’d been there” this year as the ceremony appeared to descend into chaos.

Clarkson claims the “absolute highlight” of the evening came when Tourettes sufferer Davidson shouted a racist slur during the ceremony.

The critically acclaimed film I Swear tells the true story of activist Davidson, who was present to watch the actor portraying him collect a Best Actor award.



During the ceremony, Davidson shouted out a racial slur as Sinners stars Jordan and Lindo presented a gong – triggering what Clarkson described as a nightmare scenario for BBC editors.

And Clarkson has admitted that he would have given anything to be inside the edit suite as staff scrambled to prepare the show for transmission within two hours.

He told The Times: “I would give my entire n**sack to have been in there with them, not to contribute to the debate, but to watch a room full of luvvies tie themselves in knots.

“They should make a movie about it one day. It could be called’When Bandwagons Collide’. It’d probably win a Bafta.”

Producers had already sparked outrage after cutting a clip in which one winner shouted “Free Palestine“.



But the Tourettes outburst, Clarkson suggested, posed a far trickier dilemma.

The former Top Gear man said the entire point of I Swear was to highlight the reality of Tourette syndrome, meaning the tic was part of that reality.

However, he questioned whether it was acceptable to broadcast a white man using a racial slur – even if he could not help it due to uncontrollable tics.

Clarkson admitted the BBC was left facing an impossible choice – include the clip and risk broadcasting racism, or cut it and risk being accused of discrimination against disabled people.

Clarkson said it demonstrated what he called the “nightmare world” created by the left, adding he would not say what he would have done himself because he too would end up apologising for weeks.



And he noted that that whichever route the BBC chose, apologies would inevitably follow.

He added: “If they left it in they’d be broadcasting racism. If they took it out? That would be disablist.

“It’d be like pixelating someone’s wheelchair. Only one thing is for certain. Whether they left it in, or took it out, they would have to apologise to someone.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

In the end, the BBC left the clip in and apologised the following morning to black viewers who had been offended.

Clarkson then quipped how the BBC had to “apologise to Tourettes sufferers for issuing the apology”.

News.title: The string of mystery suicides rocking world’s ‘sex capital’… as victims are chillingly dubbed the ‘Pattaya Flying Club’
News.content:
WITH its countless gogo bars and massage parlours, Pattaya seems like paradise for single men with money in their pocket.

But the neon-lit façade of easy sex and non-stop partying – which has seen Thailand’s Sin City dubbed ‘Porno Disneyland’ – hides a grim and tragic secret.



The suicide rate among foreigners who move to live there is five times the national average, with many taking the same horrific route of jumping from a high-rise balcony.



It has become such a common phenomenon that the method has even acquired its own sickening nickname – the Pattaya Flying Club.

ASOS co-founder Quentin Griffiths, 58, was the latest Brit to acquire ‘membership’ of this club when he plunged to his death from his 17th floor balcony earlier this month.

The investigation into his death, which came amid a bitter custody battle with his ex-wife and claims by her that he had stolen £500,000 from their company, is ongoing.

But a Sun investigation has revealed that the problem goes far beyond the most recent headlines.

While some visitors to Thailand’s ‘Sin City’ enjoy their stay, others end up depressed, lonely and broke.



And when the money runs out and the fun stops, some feel there is only one, tragic way out.

Alan Andrews, 68, is one of those who escaped Pattaya unscathed after he emigrated to Thailand from Port Talbot, south Wales.

He now lives in a more remote province in southern Thailand where he keeps himself busy by fishing and volunteering with the police and community groups.

But he knows plenty of others who have failed to turn their back on Pattaya and all it offers.



He said: “I had to get out of Pattaya. Staying there will kill you. The city chews up people and spits them out with empty pockets.

“If you’re coming here as a two-week millionaire, so be it. Have a great time. But if you want to survive long term, avoid the bars. The girls will bleed you dry.

“If you can’t afford that lifestyle anymore, it’s easy to become depressed.



“It’s important not to get sucked into the bar scene, as you will end up lonely.

“Many people move to Pattaya and then have nothing to do, twiddling their thumbs all day and going to the bar at night for companionship.

“I know dozens who come thinking it’s paradise, but in six months they’re skint.

“Many Brits fall in love with a Thai bar girl and it all goes wrong. They spend their life savings building houses for them and buying them a car and they then get kicked out and she returns to her local boyfriend.

“For every one happily married foreigner, there are at least 20 who have lost everything.”



One Brit told The Sun of his personal experience of how the promise of paradise can quickly turn sour.

He said: “I loved the party lifestyle of Pattaya, hitting the bars on a Saturday night, finishing the night with a girl, a third of the price of what they would cost in London.

“I remember taking a beautiful bar girl home for an evening and in the space of a few hours was enamoured.



“She said she loved me and that she was saving her money to buy a car. The next day, I saw her social media update – posing in front of a brand new Toyota arm-in-arm with her Thai boyfriend.

“My evening’s pay – a hundred pounds or so – had tipped the balance, and they had reached the amount they needed for a car.

“She never worked in the bar again. I was devastated, but realised it’s all about money for them.”

‘With no way out, they jump’

British businessman Steven Acreman, who runs a number of bars in the resort, echoed the dangers of British men falling for pretty, much younger Thai women, only to have their hearts broken.

He said: “The reasons for jumping usually revolve around money – having everything financially taken away by a Thai girlfriend or wife. I know many people who have been ripped off.



“Untreated mental illness is also a problem. I see them in Pattaya a lot, people who shouldn’t be in the country but have no money to fly home.

“Some simply do not want to go home. Pattaya is addictive and when the money runs out people do crazy things to stay.

“That snowballs into dangerous situations and with no way out they jump.”



Deaths of people jumping from high balconies occur at a frightening rate.

At one stage there were so many people falling from one building near the seafront Central shopping mall that nets were put up.

A review in 2024 found one foreigner was dying by this method every five days and in the summer of that year five fell to their deaths in less than a week.

Brit Martin Upton, 69, was one of those to die in 2024, falling from a fourth-floor hotel balcony in the early hours of a Sunday morning.



He died from his injuries in hospital on the same day that a 60-year-old Swedish swimming coach died in a fall from a hotel elsewhere in the city.

In September last year Keith Jones, 74, of Prestatyn, Wales, also died after falling from his 10th floor hotel room.

We have so much temptation at our fingertips, it is very easy – especially if you’re not disciplined – to get carried away Trevor Knight

And in October 2023, Brit Fred Adamson, 89, plunged to his death from the fifth floor of an apartment block, with his body being found next to the complex’s swimming pool.

Although the vast majority of these deaths are clearly suicides, the odd one gives rise to suspicion.

In 2019 there were calls for Thai police to investigate the death of ex-British Steel worker John Toms, 68, of Neath, Wales, who had been involved in an acrimonious divorce from his Thai wife.



He ran up huge debts after “spending a fortune” on his former sex worker wife who he then discovered was cheating on him, according to allegations contained in court papers linked to the divorce.

Pals feared he had been murdered before being pushed off the balcony to make it look like a suicide but no one was ever convicted in relation to his death.

Another Brit told of a similar case: “My friend supposedly jumped from a balcony. Two hours before that he called me to tell me he was fine, on the way to the shops.

“All of a sudden, he’s dead. He was also in the middle of a very bitter court case with his ex-wife. I do not believe he killed himself.”



There has been no documented case of a murder conviction in relation to a balcony death in Pattaya.

Brits are often victims of such deaths but it is a global phenomenon.

Last summer, a 70-year-old South Korean man plunged to his death from his hotel balcony two days after a Chinese man died in similar circumstances.



And in October last year, American Alan Pryor, 70, who lived alone, was found dead below his 5th floor condo.

He had left a note that read: “I am leaving because I have run out of money.”

The ‘Pattaya Flying Club’

Brit Trevor Knight documents life in Pattaya on his Buzzin’ Pattaya YouTube channel.

He said: “The Pattaya Flying Club, as we call it, derives from the sad situation where people run out of money and have little if any other options out of their predicament.

“Many people come out here, particularly men, with great expectations that they will be living the dream.



“We have so much temptation at our fingertips, it is very easy – especially if you’re not disciplined – to get carried away and caught up in the motion of the environment we live in.

“Many men fall in love with the beautiful women surrounding us and unfortunately for some that relationship is very tainted and all about financial gains from her side.

“We see guys with X amount of money to live with and survive, but after falling in love he overspends and runs out of money.



“As we all know, when the money disappears, so does the woman in 99.9 per cent of relationships here.

“Now the man is left heartbroken and penniless and with no options to get himself back on his feet.

“When you reach despair and with no hope of finding a solution, unfortunately people decide to take matters into their own hands, hence the Flying Club.

“When your dreams are shattered and your heart is broken, desperate times call for desperate measures.”

How to get help EVERY 90 minutes in the UK a life is lost to suicide It doesn’t discriminate, touching the lives of people in every corner of society – from the homeless and unemployed to builders and doctors, reality stars and footballers. It’s the biggest killer of people under the age of 35, more deadly than cancer and car crashes. And men are three times more likely to take their own life than women. Yet it’s rarely spoken of, a taboo that threatens to continue its deadly rampage unless we all stop and take notice, now. If you, or anyone you know, needs help dealing with mental health problems, the following organisations provide support: CALM, www.thecalmzone.net, 0800 585 858

Heads Together,www.headstogether.org.uk

HUMEN www.wearehumen.org

Mind, www.mind.org.uk, 0300 123 3393

Papyrus, www.papyrus-uk.org, 0800 068 41 41

Samaritans,www.samaritans.org, 116 123

Deadly ‘difficulties’

Local police figures show that five per cent of deaths of foreigners in Pattaya are by suicide, compared to a national suicide rate of just over one per cent.



Police Colonel Anek Sarathongyu, Superintendent of Pattaya City Police Station, said: “One of the main factors behind foreigners jumping from buildings is personal stress.

“We do not always know what they may be going through. It could be problems with colleagues, business partners, family issues from their home country, health problems, or depression. There are usually many factors involved.

“When officers examine the scene we often find anti-anxiety medication, antidepressants, or medicine for existing medical conditions.

“In many cases, we also find alcohol or substances such as cannabis in the room of the deceased.

“We cannot know who may be facing personal difficulties.”



Thai authorities are making efforts to combat the problem by establishing a suicide taskforce with input from the Department of Mental Health, the police and social media influencers.

The innovative approach uses the influencers to monitor social media round the clock to identify expressions of suicidal thoughts.



The Hope (Helper of Psychiatric Emergency) Taskforce can then launch a rapid intervention, with police officers attending the home address of the person at risk in extreme cases.

The project has more than 600 successful interventions to its name and in 2023 was awarded the Thailand Public Service Award.

Admirable though their efforts are, the Thai authorities still have some way to go to put an end to the so-called Flying Club.

Just this week, Tomasz Piechura, a Polish national in his 30s, was found dead after falling from his condo in central Pattaya.

He had left his job working for BMW in Poland to move to Thailand so he could follow his dream of becoming a Muay Thai fighter.



Sadly, just like so many other foreigners before him, his dream move to the Land of Smiles ended in a tragic nightmare.

News.title: M62 closed in BOTH directions after crash as air ambulance lands on motorway with drivers facing hour-long delays
News.content:
A MAJOR motorway has been shut in both directions after a “serious collision”.

The M62 is closed in both directions after the accident between junction 20 at Rochdale and junction 19 at Heywood.





Emergency crews, including cops, raced to the scene after the crash.

National Highways traffic officers are also in attendance to help manage the situation.

An Air Ambulance also attended the scene, but it is unclear at this time if anyone was injured.

Motorists were brought to a slow crawl on their journeys after the crash.



All traffic appears to be held in both directions, with cameras showing an empty carriageway on one side and stationary traffic on the other.

The westbound carriageway was closed completely while the eastbound carriageway was blocked, said National Highways.

National Highways reports four miles of congestion leading up to the incident, with delays of up to 60 minutes in the area.

Pictures showed cars bumper to bumper as the closure sparked traffic chaos on Sunday afternoon.



Drivers were also urged to allow extra time for their journeys today.

Anyone planning to use the major motorway is urged to re-route or even delay travel if possible.

An update on the National Highways site reads: “North West Motorway Police Group have requested a closure westbound between J20 and J19.

“It is anticipated that accident investigations will be required.”

National Highways said their 24/7 contact centre team is also available to provide up to the minute information on 0300 123 5000.

News.title: 90s football pinups now from secret affairs to cancer and drug abuse
News.content:
90s football pinups now from secret affairs to cancer and drug abuse

They say a footballer’s time on the turf is famously fleeting, but for these 90s legends, the real action only began once they hung up their boots. From vile family betrayals to serious criminal allegations, these sports stars have never known the quiet life

View 8 Images The media boom of football, the nineties ballers walked so Beckham could run. (Image: GETTY)

The 1990s was an incredible era for British football, that introduced us to a golden generation of sports stars who shattered records and banked fortunes we can only dream of.

‌



But while their trophies might have glittered, the headlines off the pitch often told a different story. From courtrooms and controversial interviews, to family fallouts and public spats, some of the Premier League's biggest names have found that fame doesn't just magnify brilliance, it highlighted scandal too.

‌



From Ryan Giggs and Robbie Fowler to Michael Owen and Cristiano Ronaldo, these footballer heavyweights have all discovered that life beyond the touchline can generate headlines of its own.

‌



Ian Huntley's chilling letter to daughter after she discovered who he was READ MORE :

Ryan Giggs

View 8 Images Ryan Giggs (Image: Bob Thomas/Getty Images)

Once the most decorated player in Premier League history, Ryan Giggs, 52, synonymous with Manchester United's glory days, has largely withdrawn from the spotlight. The Red Devils legend is experiencing fatherhood again in his fifties, far removed from the dressing rooms that defined his life.

But Giggs remains a divisive figure. His distinguished playing career has frequently been overshadowed by personal scandal - most notoriously his affair with his brother Rhodri's wife, Natasha. The eight year affair happened while Ryan was still married to and had children with Stacey Cooke.

‌



In an interview given to the James English podcast in 2021, Rhodri told how it took him five years to get over the betrayal, adding: "Ryan deserved to be on a pedestal because of his career. But he ruined his legacy by doing what he did," he said.

View 8 Images Rhodri Giggs and Natasha (Image: MDM)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

‌



But he has since reconciled with his brother - and even joked about profiting from the situation, quipping earlier this year: "Mate, it was over 14 years ago and I've got two kids with someone else and made s*** load of money from it."

In 2023, Giggs was cleared of assault allegations relating to his ex-girlfriend Kate Greville after she chose not to give evidence in a retrial. He denied all charges.

Since then he's been rebuilding. In 2024, his girlfriend Zara Charles, 36, a lingerie model, gave birth to their daughter Cora. He also took on a role as director of football at Salford City, a League Two club he formerly co-owned with his Class of '92 colleagues, before stepping away last September.

‌



Robbie Fowler

View 8 Images Robbie Fowler (Image: Getty)

Fowler was Liverpool's natural finisher - but for many, one moment defines him. In April 1999, after denying rumours that he had a drug habit, Fowler responded to taunts from Everton fans by kneeling on the turf and pretending to sniff the touchline.

Manager Gerald Houllie tried to resell the act as "eating grass," but the FA wasn't amused, and Fowler was fined £32,000.

‌



Speaking out more recently, the 50-year-old, who is reportedly worth £38million, told of the pain that the constant rumours had caused him, adding: "I was even having people writing on the wall outside our home that I was a smackhead or I was this or that. So, it got to a point where it was all really embarrassing.”

View 8 Images Robbie Fowler now (Image: Getty Images)

Fowler has spoken more recently about a health scare. In December last year, he revealed he'd had an operation to remove skin cancer from his face.

‌



Urging others to get checked out, Fowler said: "Go and get those blemishes/spots checked, basal cell carcinoma, caught early thankfully so all good." The NHS describes basal cell carcinoma as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer that starts in the top layer of the skin.

Michael Owen

View 8 Images Michael Owen (Image: Ross Kinnaird/ALLSPORT)

Married England football legend Michael Owen faced scrutiny after the Mirror revealed he had begged a reality TV star to send him 20 topless photographs, telling her he would "judge" them. The ex-striker, exchanged hundreds of text messages with former Big Brother contestant Rebecca Jane, including numerous explicit remarks.

‌



During one journey away from home, he requested additional photographs from Rebecca, writing: "Come on then, fire them through. It's a long boring trip to London so I need something to do... leave nothing to the imagination. And I'll advise on the suitability of them for social media – hopefully none of them are suitable."

When Rebecca complied, he responded: "Tremendous by the way." Eager for more, he pressed: "Surely you've got pics saved up. I thought I was going to be judging dozens?"

View 8 Images Michael Owen now (Image: Getty Images)

‌



Following another image, he remarked: "Well you went straight in there with a pic of your t**s so there's no point taking a back step now. Hmmm. Not bad. Great t**. Definitely a no-go for social media. How about send me about 20? That'd be good."

In a separate exchange, Rebecca shared a photograph of herself in the bath – despite him mentioning he was sat in the living room with his family. Owen tied the knot with his childhood love Louise Bonsall back in June 2005, with photographs featured in Hello! magazine.

Owen became a national hero with his sensational individual strike against Argentina at the 1998 World Cup. He netted 40 goals across 89 appearances for England.

‌



He remains a prominent figure in football as a pundit. Meanwhile his daughter Gemma Owen came second on Love Island with Luca Bish, though the pair didn't remain in a relationship.

She brutally labelled her dad as a "weirdo" after he admitted that has only ever watched 13 films in his entire life.

"He's awful with films. He can't sit through them. He just starts fidgeting and then he's off. He can't keep still for that long," she said on on On Emily Dean's Walking The Dog podcast.

‌



When asked why she thought her dad had such a dislike of films, she added: "I don't know. He's a weirdo isn't he."

Cristian o Ronaldo

View 8 Images Wayne Rooney with Cristiano Ronaldo during their time playing together for Manchester United (Image: PA)

A global icon, Ronaldo's career has been as headline-heavy as it has been trophy-laden. Recently he gifted fiancée Georgina Rodriguez a 30-carat diamond engagement ring said to be worth around $30 million.

‌



But the headlines haven't all been so positive. In 2025, he faced criticism after appearing at the White House and posting a photo with President Donald Trump captioned: "Two GOATS".

He has also faced a long-running legal battle over allegations that the former Manchester United and Real Madrid star sexually assaulted Kathryn Mayorga in a Las Vegas hotel penthouse suite 15 years ago. He emphatically denied her claims after the pair were snapped at a nightclub.

In June last 2022, a Las Vegas court dismissed her federal civil case against the Portuguese star.

‌



Now 41, Ronaldo is approaching the twilight of his remarkable playing career. With an astounding net worth of approximately £900million, the Portuguese forward is considering what's next.

He famously lambasted the Glazers in his explosive interview with Piers Morgan which led to the end of his second spell at the club in 2022.

Two years ago, he hinted that he could fix their problems by becoming their owner, saying: "The problem of Manchester United is the same. The problem is not always the coach. It's much more than that. If I will be the owner of the club, I will make things clear and adjust things that are bad there."

News.title: World’s sixth richest man is involved in F1 and leaves Lewis Hamilton in the dust
News.content:
World’s sixth richest man is involved in F1 and leaves Lewis Hamilton in the dust

The start of the Formula 1 season is just a few weeks away and, as always, the sport will bring together some of the world's richest people

View 3 Images Lewis Hamilton is F1's richest driver (Image: Kym Illman, Getty Images)

The new Formula 1 season is merely weeks away. Like every year, one of the most prestigious sports on the globe will unite some of the wealthiest individuals in the world on a weekly basis as they compete on the circuit and in the paddock.

‌



Whilst F1 features numerous affluent drivers and team principals, there's one individual whose remarkable net worth surpasses F1 legend Lewis Hamilton and Aston Martin proprietor Lawrence Stroll.

‌



Larry Ellison, chairman, CEO and co-founder of software behemoth Oracle - Red Bull's title sponsor - occupies the leading position among F1's wealthiest billionaires, according to Crash's list. He has a staggering net worth of £140billion, per Forbes.

‌



MAKE THE MIRROR YOUR FIRST CHOICE! Click here to activate or add us as Preferred Source in your Google search settings

He is presently the sixth wealthiest person on the planet, trailing the likes of Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos and Mark Zuckerberg. He served as Oracle's CEO from 1977 to 2014 and now operates as its chief technology officer.

Ellison was spotted in the Red Bull Racing garage during the Miami GP in May last year, alongside Musk. He possesses numerous spectacular cars and is believed to be close with US President Donald Trump.

‌



Red Bull car catches fire at F1 fan event as worried crowd warn driver READ MORE :

For context, Ellison's enormous fortune makes him roughly 400 times wealthier than Hamilton, who is valued at a reported £350million as F1's richest driver.

Carlos Slim Helu takes the second spot on the list. Known as Mexico's wealthiest individual and the billionaire benefactor who has backed much of F1 star Sergio Perez's career from 2011 to 2024. Perez is now back on the grid with Cadillac in 2026.

Slim currently ranks as the 16th richest person globally, boasting a reported net worth of approximately £87bn, according to Forbes. A business tycoon, investor, and philanthropist, his company, Telmex, has long sponsored Perez.

‌



View 3 Images Larry Ellison is thought to be close with US President Donald Trump (Image: Getty)

Mark Mateschitz is the third wealthiest billionaire in F1, with a fortune of £34bn. The son of the late Red Bull founder Dietrich, Mateschitz ranks 38th on Forbes's list of the world's richest people as of 2025.

The Austrian billionaire owns 49% of Red Bull GmbH, the energy drink firm. Piero Ferrari then ranks as F1's fourth richest billionaire.

‌



The Italian businessman has a net worth of £5.7bn, according to Forbes. Ferrari, the only living son of iconic founder Enzo, holds a 10.48% stake in the Ferrari automotive company, where he serves as vice chairman.

View 3 Images Lawrence Stroll is one of the sport's wealthiest men (Image: Getty)

As of this year, he is the 304th richest person globally and the fifth wealthiest in his home country. Aramco F1 Team's owner, Stroll, occupies the fifth spot with a net worth of £3.7bn.

‌



In 2018, the Canadian businessman led a group of investors in purchasing the assets of the defunct Force India Formula One team.

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! Latest news, analysis and much more on Mirror Football's Facebook page

For the 2019 season, the team name was altered to Racing Point F1 Team, with Stroll's son, Lance, piloting one of the cars. In 2020, it was revealed that Stroll headed the Yew Tree Investments consortium to inject £182m into Aston Martin, in exchange for a 16.7% stake in the company.

Article continues below

The team was rebranded as the Aston Martin F1 Team in 2021. The 2026 F1 season will kick off with the Australian Grand Prix in Melbourne on March 6.

News.title: Iran attacks: Gulf states dragged into US-Israel strike aftermath amid 9/11 warning
News.content:
With tensions escalating in the Middle East, experts have warned that we have entered the most important period in military history since the 9/11 attacks.

Countries across the Middle East have been drawn into the Israeli-US conflict with Iran whose missiles and drones targeted Cyprus, Dubai, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Qatar. US bases and key military and economic targets were hit. Iran's First Vice President, Mohammad Reza Aref, warned of 'the most devastating offensive operation' in retaliation for airstrikes that killed the country's leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and four of his family.

More than 200 Israeli fighter jets were used, marking the largest deployment in its air force's history. Israel struck about 500 targets, including anti-aircraft batteries and missile launch sites.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings

‌



View 8 Images Missiles over Dubai (Image: SWNS)

Dubai airport strike: Brit mum sunbathing when missiles started flying stranded with kids READ MORE :

Thousands of Brits were forced to run for cover in five and six-star hotels in Dubai as Iran's missiles sent debris flying. The attacks caused flight chaos, stranding tens of thousands of travellers. UK nationals were told to register with the Foreign Office for the latest advice. US and British citizens were told to remain indoors for safety.

Here is what we know so far about the situation in each country and why Iran targeted the Gulf states:

Dubai

Disturbing scenes unfolded at the city's International Airport (DXB) as passengers ran for cover while missiles rained down from the sky. The world's busiest airport by passenger traffic sustained damage in an "incident" that injured four staff members. Thousands of flights to and from the region were grounded, marking one of the most serious disruptions to global travel since the Covid-19 pandemic.

‌



Military action across the Middle East impacted 18,000 flights on Saturday. Iran has vowed 'the most devastating offensive operation' against US bases and Israel in retaliation, despite losing armed forces chief of staff Abdolrahim Mousavi, killed in airstrikes. Videos posted on social media showed smoke filling the terminal at the airport, one of the world's busiest. Around 90,000 passengers pass through the main airports in Abu Dhabi, Dubai and Doha every day.

View 8 Images Fire at the Fairmont The Palm hotel on Saturday (Image: X)

Fairmont The Palm, a luxury Dubai hotel on the emirate's manmade island, caught fire after an object was seen falling from the sky. Dubai's government confirmed four people had been injured but did not say whether it was the result of a direct missile attack or an interception.

‌



Other images showed parts of Dubai airport deserted after passengers were forced to evacuate the damaged terminal. Debris from an "aerial interception" caused a fire in a berth at the Jebel Ali deep sea port, the world's ninth busiest, in Dubai.

The city's resident Becky Williams could hear missiles fired by UAE authorities aimed at intercepting incoming Iranian projectiles. "I probably saw about 15 missiles being launched from behind my house," she said. "You can hear the interceptions happening in the air."

Emirates has temporarily suspended its operations to and from Dubai. Lufthansa, Air India, Virgin Atlantic and Turkish Airlines have also announced cancellations. Some countries in the region, including Iraq and Jordan, closed their airspace.

Many celebrities and influencers, including TV presenter Vicky Pattison, were caught up in the crossfire. Three people were killed in the attacks involving 165 ballistic missiles and 541 bomb-carrying drones; 506 drones were destroyed.

‌



View 8 Images Smoke and flames over Dubai (Image: UGC/ANONYMOUS/AFP via Getty Imag)

Abu Dhabi

Ex-Rangers and Liverpool manager Graeme Souness posted two videos on Instagram from Zayed International Airport in Abu Dhabi as he attempted to return to the UK. In the first video he said: "I'm hearing some pretty loud bangs go off and that is anti-missiles taking out missiles that have been fired at some American bases."

An update then followed, with the 72-year-old saying: "Flight's been cancelled, still hearing explosions so we've given up, we're just going to check into a hotel for a couple of nights and see where we are. Hopefully things will settle down and we'll get back on the flight. You get grumpy when you get delays and stuff at airports but I think we should think of people that maybe this time are dying."

An Iranian drone targeted had targeted the airport terminal, and falling debris killed one person and injured seven. Iran also targeted key economic areas, including oil installations.

‌



View 8 Images Pakistan was also drawn into conflict with demo at US Embassy (Image: AP)

Experts believe the attacks targeted densely populated residential areas to force leaders into talks with the US. They want Arab leaders to persuade the US to stop the attacks.

Al Dhafra was the key target because it is the main US military base in the country. State media reported that the UAE said it has closed its airspace "partially and temporarily" as a precaution.

Cyprus

The holiday island hugely popular with British holidaymakers became a target for Iran because it is also a key intelligence gathering post. Around 1.3m Brits travel to Cyprus every year, accounting for about 30 percent of total visitors. There is also a British military base and the 'Five Eyes' monitoring station which provides vital intelligence on Iran to the UK, US, Canada, New Zealand, and Australia.

‌



UK Defence Secretary John Healey confirmed two missiles had been launched toward Cyprus and expressed concerns for British Army servicemen there. The travel disruption, the most significant since the Covid 19 pandemic, triggered by the conflict will have a huge impact on Cyprus if it continues. The Defence Secretary revealed that two Iranian missiles were fired in the direction of Cyprus.

John Healey said it was not yet clear whether the missiles deliberately targeted UK facilities on the island. Speaking to Sky News, he added, “Few people will mourn the Ayatollah’s death.”

View 8 Images Defence Secretary John Healey warned of danger to UK personnel on Cyprus (Image: PA)

‌



However, he stressed his greater concern was the potential fallout from the strikes on Iran and the risk of wider regional escalation. He noted the 20 terror plots on the UK’s streets organised by Iran, the death of tens of thousands of its own citizens and the supply of 50,000 drones to Russia to attack Ukraine.

“This is a regime which...can never be allowed to have a nuclear weapon," he added. Cyprus government spokesman Constantinos Letymbiotis posted on X that reports suggesting missiles had been fired toward Cyprus are not valid and that “there is no indication whatsoever that any threat to the country has occurred.”

Qatar

Smoke rose into the skies above Doha following the Iranian missile strikes on US military compounds and other targets. The city has become a major Middle East transport hub and has grown popular with Brits since the 2022 World Cup. A trapped UK family recounted their nerve-racking experience.

Jo Hummel, 43, and her husband Chris Jenkins, 46, were travelling back from Thailand with their two children, Ivy, 17, and Kit, 12, when their connecting flight to London was cancelled. Jo, from the Isle of Wight, said: "We touched down in Qatar, and people were opening up their phones to see a national alert coming through in Arabic. We realised something was going on. We were transferring in Doha to London and everything was getting cancelled."

‌



View 8 Images Missile debris rained down on the busy streets of Doha (Image: X)

She continued: "Qatar Airways staff came and offered us a hotel room. At first, we were going to stay at the airport, but now we are 20 minutes away in a hotel organised by the airline. I had to get the kids out of bed this morning because drones were exploding in the sky above us, and we have big windows. It's quite frightening. As a mother, I'm scared out of my wits and trying to keep calm, but also be organised."

Brits are likely to be stranded for some time; the airlines serving the region have suspended all flights as they assess safety. Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that its forces responded to the strikes by hitting multiple sites in Israel as well as five major US military facilities:

Al Udeid airbase in Qatar, Al Dhafra airbase in the UAE, the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain, Ali Al Salem airbase in Kuwait, and Muwaffaq Salti airbase in Jordan. They said a US FP-132 radar system in Qatar had been "completely destroyed.Iran also warned that "no ship would be allowed to pass the Strait of Hormuz" in the Gulf, through which about 20% of global oil and gas shipments pass.

‌



Bahrain

The Government disclosed that around 300 British military personnel were stationed close to targeted sites there. Missile strikes hit key US military targets, sending smoke rising into the air. British residents reported receiving regular updates from Bahrain's authorities as they assessed the level of danger from the strikes.

View 8 Images The Crowne Plaza Manama hotel in Bahrain came under attack (Image: X)

"They are targeting military sites so I feel safe as I am not in the vicinity," one told the BBC. "But there are those who are looking to leave."

‌



The Interior Ministry said the airport was damaged after being targeted by a drone

Kuwait

The country's Ministry of Health reported that one person was killed and 20 people were wounded in the latest retaliatory attacks by Iran. The country’s news agency reported the latest toll.

None of the casualties are Kuwaiti citizens, the ministry said. A dozen people were injured in previous attacks in Kuwait on Saturday. The Kuwaiti army said Sunday that it destroyed several ballistic missiles and drones launched against the Gulf country “since the start of the Iranian aggression.

View 8 Images A drone struck Kuwait International Airport damaging Terminal 1 (Image: X)

‌



Their defence ministry said in a statement that 97 Iranian ballistic missiles launched toward the State of Kuwait were detected. Debris falling on facilities led to “minor material damage."

Pakistan

Six people were reportedly killed and three US Embassy staff members were injured in Karachi. Gunfire erupted near the US Consulate General there after hundreds of people stormed the building in the southern port city.

Images showed the crowds attacking the embassy's thick glass windows with makeshift weapons.

Protesters threw stones at police during the violent unrest. Police and paramilitary forces used batons and fired tear gas to disperse the crowd, said Mohammad Jawad, a police official.

‌



At least one protester was killed and several others were wounded in clashes between protesters and security forces, he said. Officials reported that at least six people were killed and eight wounded in those clashes.

The crowd was said to be made up of Shiite Muslims, according to reports, the same branch of Islam that Ayatollah Ali Khamenei belonged to, with roughly 200–260 million adherents around the world.

News.title: UK engages in Iran war as RAF Typhoon fighter 'takes out' drone
News.content:
The UK has engaged in the raging conflict in the Middle East.

The UK's Ministry of Defence confirmed an RAF fighter jet downed an Iranian drone heading towards Qatar.

This is the first report of a British aircraft shooting down a missile or drone since the escalation in violence. UK military jets have been flying defensive missions across the region since Saturday when the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran, which promted retaliatory attacks from Tehran on targets throughout the Middle East.

Confirming the action, a Ministry of Defence spokesperson said: “On 1 March 2026, a Royal Air Force Typhoon operating from Qatar as part of the joint UK–Qatar Typhoon Squadron, successfully took out an Iranian drone heading towards Qatari territory.

Image: AFP via Getty Images) AFP via Getty Images)

“The Typhoon jet was conducting a defensive air patrol and used an air‑to‑air missile to shoot down the drone, ensuring the security of Qatar’s airspace and British interests in the region. Our Armed Forces are playing a vital role to protect our people, our interests and our Allies.”

The destruction of the drone represents the joint squadron's first operational interception in defence of Qatar.

Armed Forces Minister Al Carns praised the RAF for the interception. Writing on X, he said: "The professionalism of our RAF is remarkable….good work…keep it going."

Image: AFP via Getty Images) AFP via Getty Images)

It comes after a British counter-drone unit in Iraq destroyed an Iranian drone that was heading towards a coalition base housing UK service personnel. RAF Typhoons and F-35s have also been flying defensive missions out of a British base in Cyprus.

Two Iranian missiles have been fired in the direction of the Mediterranean island, where thousands of UK personnel are stationed, although the Government does not believe these were deliberately targeted at the British bases there.

The Government has mounted an unprecedented operation to support hundreds of thousands of British nationals in the Gulf amid continued Iranian attacks..

News.title: Moment US air strike takes out Iran troops as they prepare to fire missile
News.content:
Moment US air strike takes out Iran troops as they prepare to fire missile

Aerial footage released by Benjamin Netanyahu's military captured the moment they 'thwarted a launch' by Iranian soldiers allegedly targeted at Israel

Iranian troops struck down by drone before firing missile

This is the incredible moment Iranian troops were struck down in an American and Israel operation, just moments before they were due to unleash a potentially deadly attack.

‌



The footage showed two people, who Israel claimed were soldiers of the regime, loading missile launchers in western Iran. The black and white aerial footage caught the moment these people were quickly taken out before they could unleash chaos with more strikes.

‌



The US and Israel attacked Tehran and several other Iranian cities on Saturday morning. Iran retaliated by launching it's own set of attacks, not only on Israel but also on neighbouring countries like the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Bahrain.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 5 Images Two 'Iranian soldiers' were taken out by an Israeli strike (Image: ISRAEL AIR FORCE)

Iran US LIVE: Three US service members dead and several injured in Operation Epic Fury READ MORE :

The took out Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who was killed when at least four buildings in his compound, in Tehran, were hit on Saturday. Strikes have continued today with the Israeli Air Force, in a statement, saying its defence forces were working to take out launchers which could pose a threat to its citizens.

‌



View 5 Images Israel shared footage of them supposedly preventing a strike on its people (Image: ISRAEL AIR FORCE)

The Israeli Air Force shared the footage of two "Iranian troops" being taken out at the missile launcher and shared a chilling message. It said: "As part of the Air Force strikes in western Iran, operatives from the Iranian terror regime's ground-to-ground missile unit were identified arming a launcher in western Iran and preparing it for immediate launch toward the civilians of the State of Israel.

View 5 Images Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed on Saturday (Image: Getty Images)

‌



"The IDF identified the threat and, in a rapid closing of the circle, struck the forces and the launcher, thereby thwarting a launch toward the rear of the State of Israel." In a separate message, the IDF claimed this bold move prevented a strike on Israel.

Iranian state media today confirmed the supreme leader, after nearly 37 years in power, was killed in Saturday's attacks. Several other senior officials of the horror dictatorship were also killed. These include the Iranian army's chief of staff, General Abdol Rahim Mousavi, and the defence minister General Aziz Nasirzadeh.

View 5 Images Israel has launched a new set of strike on Iran today (Image: GPO/AFP via Getty Images)

‌



The Revelotionary Guard's top commander, Major General Mohammad Pakpour, was also taken out. Iranian media added that Khamenei's daughter, son-in-law, grandchild and daughter-in-law were also killed on Saturday.

Donald Trump, on Truth Social, wrote: "Khamenei, one of the most evil people in History, is dead. This is not only Justice for the people of Iran, but for all Great Americans, and those people from many Countries throughout the World, that have been killed or mutilated by Khamenei and his gang of bloodthirsty THUGS.

View 5 Images A cloud of smoke after strikes on Tehran on Sunday (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

Article continues below

"He was unable to avoid our Intelligence and Highly Sophisticated Tracking Systems and, working closely with Israel, there was not a thing he, or the other leaders that have been killed along with him, could do."

News.title: CHRIS HUGHES: 'Killing Iran leader may be remembered as day US lost its credibility in future'
News.content:
CHRIS HUGHES: 'Killing Iran leader may be remembered as day US lost its credibility in future'

Ali Khamenei's death may see an even more violent leader emerge from the ashes of US and Israeli air strikes to take charge of one of the most oppressed societies in the world

View 10 Images After Ali Khamenei's death, a new leadership could emerge that is even more dangerous (Image: AP)

America's war on Iran may have decapitated the regime’s oppressive leadership, but the powerful exporters of violence who emerge from the ashes could be far worse.

‌



The UK’s defence secretary John Healey is right to suggest few will mourn the death of Khamenei who led an ‘evil regime’ through decades of murderous oppression. His main policy was the destruction of Israel, ‘death to America’ and to rule with a hangman’s noose one of the most oppressed societies in the World.

‌



But out of the rubble left by American and Israeli air strikes, what may take over is unlikely to be a western-friendly, fluffy and liberal democracy that the US and UK can do business with.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 10 Images A cloud of smoke after strikes on Tehran on Sunday (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

Russia threatens horror strikes on Europe in response to Iran leader's death READ MORE :

Already the powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps are fighting back, striking countries all over the Middle East and beyond, revealing a new hunger for chaos and spreading mayhem. This lashing out at anyone with western links reveals a doomsday policy of Iran’s failing regime, dragging the rest of the region into its misery - whilst pointing the figure at Israel and the US.

‌



It could be a military junta that takes over, made up of former spies and shadowy Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps generals, zealots devoted to spreading Iranian power. It is believed IRGC leaders have been killed, meaning those loyalists left will be spurred towards revenge and the group has proved to be incredibly good at exporting terrorism.

View 10 Images People mourn the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after he was killed in joint US and Israeli strikes (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

And yet Trump tells them carelessly: “Lay down your arms or face certain death” even though they are facing death in reprisals for their treatment of the internal opposition. Al-Qaeda figures, relatives of 9-11 plotter Osama bin Laden have been given shelter in Iran by officials even though they lie on opposite sides of the Islamic Sunni-Shia schism.

‌



The stronger mutual attraction is their mutual hatred of the US and its allies such as the UK, a junior partner, now much more under threat from terrorism emanating from Iran. Terror groups detached from their big supporter Tehran such Hezbollah, The Yemeni Houthis, Iraqi pro-Iranian militia have all come under attack or face oblivion.

View 10 Images Motorists drive past a plume of smoke rising from a reported Iranian strike in the industrial district of Doha. (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

So with nothing to lose they will likely turn on the west and Iran could face the same kind of insurgency from former now disenfranchised military officers- as happened in Iraq. And the albeit accidental hit on an Iranian school in the south killing at least a believed 148 is a blood-soaked tragedy that will haunt this operation in decades to come.

‌



This was one of the most ill-thought out, legally dodgy attacks on a foreign country that has been launched by the United States in many decades. For one that is so breath-takingly sensitive, complex and with such immense consequences for the world it was dealt with, using all the subtlety of …a bomb on the head of its leader.

View 10 Images People look at smoke rising from the port of Jebel Ali after a reported Iranian strike in Dubai on March 1, 2026. (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

And in the decades to come the toppling of Khamenei may well be remembered as the day the US lost its credibility as a democracy that can lead negotiations on ending wars. That is because it has just led negotiations to end hostility and then punctuated the debate with a deluge of cruise missiles and ballistic missiles on the very people it was talking to.

‌



And yet Trump refuses to do anything about the Kremlin, which is killing allies in Ukraine, instead pivoting towards Vladimir Putin whenever the Moscow crime boss calls. US envoys were in the middle of negotiating an agreement with Iran and even though it had not reached a favourable conclusion, the process as far as Iran was concerned was ongoing.

View 10 Images Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was Iran's supreme leader since 1989, and he was killed in a US and Israeli attack over the weekend (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Trump did not get from Congress permission to launch the attack and it has been dressed up as protection for the US and its allies. Despite the immense war-machine, a powerful armada parked in the Arabian Sea led by the carrier USS Abraham Lincoln that had gathered, there is no apparent ‘day after’ scenario.

‌



Trump merely delegated it to Iran’s opposition protesters to ‘once we have finished - take back your country’ and effectively washing his hands of a destroyed society. He argues the strikes were ‘preventive’ to stop Iran developing the capability to be a threat, which has no legal basis and the UN Security Council did not authorise it.

American intelligence suggested recently that it would take three years for Iran to build a nuclear weapon - hardly the required narrow window for a threat to be overwhelming. Only last year Trump claimed his June strikes had obliterated Iran’s nuclear programme so either he was wrong then or these strikes were aimed at helping the opposition protests.

View 10 Images Demonstrators in Iran wave Iranian flags while chanting anti-U.S. and anti-Israel slogans (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

‌



No boots on the ground are currently expected, a direct contradiction to Trump’s claim to protesters weeks ago that “help is on the way” apart from air-strikes. And one of the reasons there is no ground invasion, as happened with Iraq, is the immense volume of troops Iran has - at least 200,000 IRGC and other forces.

At the usual calculation of attack to defender ratio, this would require probably at least half a million coalition troops and there is no coalition and nor apparently are there troops. Trump’s claim on Saturday that “major combat operations are underway in Iran” was another deception as there are apparently currently no overt military assets actually “in” Iran.

View 10 Images An F/A-18E Super Hornet prepares to launch from the flight deck of Nimitz-class aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln (Image: AP)

‌



To be more precise his preposterously named Operation Epic Fury is actually “on” Iran as the strike missions are dropping bombs without a ground-operation in the country. Former director general of the London thinktank the Royal United Services Institute Karin von Hippel said: "Most analysts would say air power alone doesn't work.

“Air power needs to be combined with, usually, boots on the ground, but there's always the exception to this rule that we just don't know yet. "There always is the possibility, it's unlikely, but there is a possibility that a significant bombing campaign that decapitates a leadership that is able to disable enough of the Revolutionary Guard and militias, which that's already a challenge, could leave enough room for the street to rise up and try to do something.

View 10 Images President Trump announced on Truth Social that Iranian supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei had been killed (Image: WHITE HOUSE/AFP via Getty Images)

‌



"It's difficult, there's no real organised opposition. It's more likely to lead to looting and anarchy like we've seen in so many other places, all the way back from Panama in 1989, we saw it in Iraq we saw in 2003, we saw it in Kosovo after the Nato bombing campaign, so that's more the typical scenario but you know, things can always turn out differently. We just don't know."

Meanwhile, an "all-out war" seems unlikely after the US-Israeli strikes in Iran, but "destabilisation" in the region is likely, the head of a foreign affairs think tank has said. Some experts believe a revolution may still be empowered by the strikes.

View 10 Images People gather to stage a demonstration after the announcement that Iranian leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei had been killed in U.S.-Israeli attacks in Tehran (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

Article continues below

Asked on the BBC's Sunday With Laura Kuenssberg programme for her "best-case scenario" after the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Chatham House chief executive Bronwen Maddox said: "Best case is that the protesters begin to come out again on the streets. They find that they are not shot down, they begin to produce leaders or a leader, and realise that they can actually change the whole regime."

And the worst-case scenario? She said: "That the Revolutionary Guard are still... show themselves to be very much in control of the country, that they continue to hit other countries... which not only destabilises the region, but it encourages our country to pull away from the US and pull away from any talks about stabilising Gaza and the West Bank."

News.title: Russia threatens horror strikes on Europe in response to Iran leader's death
News.content:
The Russian president expressed his condolences for the death of Iran's supreme leader, killed by US strikes yesterday, before Moscow hinted at possible revenge attacks on Europe.

Vladimir Putin is furious with the US after American troops joined Israel in a series of strikes on Iran yesterday. It has since been confirmed Operation Epic Fury has killed Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Iran hit back by targeting US air bases across the Middle East, sending missiles to Dubai, Kuwait and Bahrain.

Russian propagandists have since threatened to carry out a revenge strike on Europe in response, pointing the blame at Donald Trump for "unwittingly creating political and psychological opportunities", reports The Express.

The Russian President described the death of Khamenei "a murder committed in cynical violation of all norms of human morality and international law”. Meanwhile, the Russian Foreign Ministry has criticised the US-Israeli operation in Iran, dubbing it a "pre-planned and unprovoked act of armed aggression against a sovereign and independent UN member state" as well as a violation of core principles in international law.

Image: AP) AP)

The ministry finds "Washington and Tel Aviv have once again embarked on a dangerous adventure that is rapidly bringing the region closer to a humanitarian, economic and, not exclude, a radiological catastrophe", adding that the countries are "covering themselves with an imaginary concern to prevent the Iranians from acquiring nuclear weapons".

Moscow said the US and Israel are using this as a ploy, claiming the joint objective is to instead "destroy the constitutional order and destroy the leadership of a state they do not like, which has refused to submit to forceful dictate and hegemonism."

Sergey Karnaukhov, a former president and CEO of The Centre for the National Interest, and Dimitri Simes, a former president and CEO of The Centre for the National Interest, also touched on Russia's next move amid the evolving conflict while blaming Europe for "waging war" against Moscow.

Image: AFP via Getty Images) AFP via Getty Images)

In the conversation with Vladimir Solovyov on channel Solovyov Live, he said: “Estonia is waging war against us. I think we should give it some thought that Mr Trump unwittingly created political and psychological opportunities for us, he untied our hands for our Supreme Commander in chief to do whatever he finds appropriate to do for the nation’s security.”

The host of the show continued to issue threats to Europe in the broadcast. Mr Solovyov said: "But we see what is happening... the only language Europe understands is the language of force, and only when the force is used against them. They saw that Oreshnik was used twice and decided they are protected in their little house. It’s clearly time to strike their little house.”

News.title: Hundreds of thousands of Brits trapped in Middle East as evacuations planned
News.content:
Hundreds of thousands of Brits trapped in Middle East as evacuations planned

Officials are drawing up a variety of plans at pace for evacuation routes by land, sea or air, The Mirror understands, with all options currently on the table

View 2 Images Tens of thousands of British people have registered their presence in the UAE (Image: AP)

Hundreds of thousands of British holidaymakers, people in transit and expats are understood to be trapped in the Middle East as Iranian missile strikes continue to rock the region.

‌



Officials are drawing up a variety of plans at pace for evacuation routes by land, sea or air, The Mirror understands, with all options currently on the table.

‌



Explosions have been heard in multiple Gulf states as Iran vowed to carry out its "most intense operation" ever in retaliation for the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. No British nationals are currently believed to have been injured or killed.

‌



The UK over the weekend launched an unprecedented operation to support British citizens stuck in the region. More than 76,000 British people have already registered their presence in the region since the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) launched a system to do so on Saturday evening.

If you are a British national in Bahrain, Israel, Kuwait, Palestine, Qatar or the United Arab Emirates, you can register your presence to receive direct updates from the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) here.

Iran US LIVE: Three US service members dead and several injured in Operation Epic Fury READ MORE :

View 2 Images Keir Starmer today expressed "deep concern" about attacks on civilian sites (Image: ANDY RAIN/POOL/EPA/Shutterstock)

‌



Almost three-quarters of those are in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and are predominantly people on holiday or on a journey through the country, as opposed to expat residents, it is understood.

In an unprecedented move, Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper and Minister for the Middle East Hamish Falconer launched the "register your presence” system at 5pm on Saturday after urgent all-day meetings. It is the earliest time during a crisis that the system has been launched and the first time it has been launched for multiple countries at the same time.

The FCDO is urging British nationals in Bahrain, Israel, Kuwait, Palestine, Qatar or the United Arab Emirates to register their presence there so it can understand where people are as it draws up possible evacuation plans. British citizens should also sign up to travel alerts, as advice is being regularly reviewed and updated. People are being advised to stay where they are and to monitor local travel advice.

‌



Some 250,000 people travel through Dubai airport every day, with connecting flights from Britain to Australia and Asia often stopping at the major travel hub. It is considered the world's busiest airport.

Ms Cooper is understood to have been at the FCDO from 7am until after 11pm on Saturday and returned to the office first thing on Sunday morning, where she is working urgently to understand the whereabouts and security of Brits in the region. Her immediate concern is of British citizens who do not have permanent homes and are stranded in hotels, it is understood. Some people were initially stuck in airports when the strikes began, but are understood to have been moved to hotels.

Ms Cooper is also understood to be pushing heavily for a diplomatic resolution to be reached as soon as possible. The Foreign Secretary held a series of calls with foreign ministers from around the world over the weekend, including those from Kuwait, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Bahrain, Qatar and Jordan.

‌



She has also spoken with G7 foreign ministers and the US Secretary of State Marco Rubio. On Saturday, Ms Cooper also gathered all of the ambassadors for the region. Ambassadors in the UAE and Qatar are in frequent contact with major airlines. It is understood no conversations have been held with Iran.

Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander, who has been in contact with airlines today, said: “The UK’s immediate priority is the safety of UK nationals in the Middle East, but I also realise how stressful the situation is for travellers facing delays and disruption. We’re closely monitoring the situation for UK aviation and shipping and working closely with UK airlines.”

Keir Starmer on Sunday expressed "deep concern" about attacks on civilian sites in the region and discussed with Middle Eastern leaders about ensuring the safety of British nationals and military personnel in the region. He told the leaders of Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait the UK stood with them "in the face of dangerous Iranian escalation" following joint US-Israeli air strikes over the weekend.

‌



Later on Sunday, Mr Starmer also spoke with the President of Cyprus and the Sultan of Oman. The PM discussed with Cyprus's leader Nikos Christodoulides "the deeply concerning impact of Iran’s indiscriminate strike action in the region, which has hit airports, ports and infrastructure – harming innocent people as a result", No10 said. Mr Starmer underscored "his full support for the security of Cyprus and the Sovereign Base Areas”.

And in a call with Oman's Sultan Haitham bin Tarik al Said, Mr Starmer "expressed the UK’s support of Oman in the face of Iran’s widespread and uncontrolled retaliation", Downing Street said. A No10 spokesman added: "They shared their concern about the safety of civilians and the rising number of injured people as a result of the strikes."

The Israeli military said it had continued to hit sites in central Tehran following the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in an airstrike on Saturday morning. Iran has vowed to retaliate with "devastating blows" and has fired missiles at many of its neighbouring countries as well as US military targets.

Article continues below

Israeli authorities reported Iranian missiles had killed nine civilians in Beit Shemesh, near Jerusalem, while the US military's Central Command said three American service members had been killed and five others severely wounded on Sunday.

Defence Secretary John Healey this morning said some 300 UK troops had been within "a few hundred yards" of an Iranian strike on a base in Bahrain. Two missiles have also been fired in the direction of Cyprus, where thousands of UK personnel are stationed, although the Government does not believe these were deliberately targeted at the British bases there.

News.title: The months of planning behind US-Israeli mission to target Iran's supreme leader
News.content: Iran knew the supreme leader was in the sights of its enemies and so the failure to identify and deal with these vulnerabilities in the intervening months suggests a deep failure for Iranian security and counterintelligence or else the ability of Israel and the US to continue to adapt their methods to find new ways of tracking.

News.title: Two ships hit near Strait of Hormuz as fears grow of oil price rises
News.content: At least two ships have been hit by unknown projectiles near the Strait of Hormuz, as Iran continues to launch strikes across the Middle East in response to an ongoing attack against it by the US and Israel.

News.title: 'It's eerie' - Dubai on edge as city comes under attack by Iran
News.content: For two days now, Dubai residents have mainly stayed indoors as their city is hit by missiles and drones - part of an attack launched by Iran across the region in response to the latest massive and ongoing attack against it by US and Israel.

News.title: At least 153 dead after reported strike on school, Iran says
News.content: Iran has blamed the US and Israel. The US military's Central Command (Centcom) said it was looking into reports of the incident, while Israel's military said it was "not aware" of any IDF operations in the area.

News.title: In maps: Strikes across Iran and the Middle East
News.content: There have been few reports of US and Israeli strikes outside Tehran on Sunday, although the Israeli military said it had destroyed two fighter aircraft in the north-western city of Tabriz. Also, US President Donald Trump said US forces had sunk nine Iranian naval ships and "largely destroyed" their naval headquarters.
